focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 6

The yard hit Elise all at once. Sour Bièvre, dye, horse piss, rotted greens, the mineral bite of lye: the morning had warmed enough to wake every smell in it. She came up under a basket handle with the quarry's chalk cold still clinging to her skin, and when the trapdoor thudded shut behind her the sky laid itself across her face so abruptly that she had to blink like a woman brought up from fever. Steam lifted off the vats and drifted low under the young leaves, turning the whole laundry yard into something half weather, half workshop.

Clemence moved into it as if she had title to the place. Her hair was pinned under the same faded red kerchief she kept darning instead of replacing, and the right pocket of her apron clicked softly with the horn buttons she cut from shirts too far gone to save. She never threw out good buttons. Cloth died, seams burst, men vanished, but a button could still earn its keep. She dropped one basket by the trough, jerked another toward Elise with her foot, and said, "If you can carry paper, you can carry wet linen."

"That seems to be your answer to most questions," Elise said.

"It keeps the day moving."

Lucie came behind them with clothespins in a flattened sardine tin, shaking it just enough to hear the rattle. She had escaped the comb halfway through the second braid, so the left side of her head was neat and the right had already started its rebellion. There was a smooth white quarry pebble in her apron pocket. Elise had seen her thumb it at night when adults talked too low and too long. This morning the child was cheerful enough to forget it.

"Mama says Monsieur Delatour carries like a martyr," Lucie announced, "and wrings like a priest."

Lucien, coming through the gate with the next basket braced on one shoulder, said...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...close to Abel's coat, making secrecy part of the gift. With it he passed a short black twist no longer than a match, tar tacky on the braid. "Keep that dry," he murmured. "If you want proper fuse and quarry powder that won't drown in chalk, come to the lime sheds past the Gentilly barrier tomorrow after dark. I'll show you what the right stuff looks like."

Abel closed his burnt fingertips over both scraps. Pride flashed across his face so nakedly it was almost indecent.

Elise saw the exchange entire: the black tar shining on Abel's thumb where the salvaged cord had smeared him that afternoon, Ferrand's quiet promotion from crate salvage to the real article, the involuntary lift in Abel's chest, Ferrand's confidence that he needn't hide from anyone who mattered. There it was at last. Not theory. Not Vautrin's appetite dressed as certainty. A meeting place, an hour, a sample small enough to pocket and big enough to kill.

Later, when the lamp was turned down and the chamber went back to chalk, damp, and breathing, Elise lay awake on her blanket with the taste of cabbage still sour on her tongue. If she wrote the quarry cleanly for Vautrin, he had enough now to break more than a shelter: Gentilly, tarred fuse in Abel's fist, and Ferrand's invitation folded inside all the deniability a provocateur could want. If she kept silent, Abel would go to the lime sheds primed for exactly the lesson Ferrand meant to teach. Warning Lucien outright carried its own cost. She had no innocent reason to know which scrap of evening talk would turn lethal by tomorrow night.

The evidence had finally taken the shape of a clock. By morning she would have to decide whether to feed Vautrin a real lead, warn Lucien without showing her hand, or shadow Abel herself if he slipped out for Gentilly.
